searchDocs.title: AI prompts for code review that work (at least for me)
What is important for me in AI review prompts​
searchDocs.pageTitle: AI prompts for code review that work (at least for me)

searchDocs.title: One comment per file​
searchDocs.pageTitle: AI prompts for code review that work (at least for me)
searchDocs.content: I've noticed that, regardless of the tool I use (Copilot CLI, Gemini CLI, Jules, droid) reviewing code directly in terminal is extremely cumbersome. The wall of text is unreadable and unmanageable. That's why it is beneficial to instruct an agent to:  Create every review comment in a separate file.Open those files in code - this trick saves a ton of time. Especially since I close those files in the IDE, as I am done with them or moving to a folder like "closed" or even deleting them to forget. Everything to keep the environment clean.  This allows me to carefully review file by file, issue by issue, and not be overwhelmed by the amount. I can also put comments in the file and ask an agent to review my comments and redo its review (sic!) based on my input, and then open the file in code again of course!

searchDocs.title: Assess your work!​
searchDocs.pageTitle: AI prompts for code review that work (at least for me)
searchDocs.content: Another important thing is to ask an Agent for assessment of its own work. This is quite a common technique, which you can find across many prompts. I've noticed that this significantly increases the quality of the results. I also love adding things like "Add 3 arguments pro your proposals and against" to some of the prompts if I want a more detailed insight.

searchDocs.title: [PROMPT] You HATE this implementation​
searchDocs.pageTitle: AI prompts for code review that work (at least for me)
searchDocs.content: The base for this one is taken from a post made by u/cleancodecrew in r/ClaudeAI, at the beginning of 2026.  Do a git diff and pretend you're a senior dev doing a code review, and you HATE this implementation. What would you criticize? What edge cases am I missing?  I found it when I didn't have access to my own prompts and I needed something to run with Google's Jules. As Jules rejects writing normal review comments on GitHub, I have developed something like this.  So this is oriented to work well in the web-only interface of Google's Jules - notice the hard break of lines, which instructs this tool to also keep lines short. Of course, you can instruct Jules to send this review as a pull request, but I never found it comfortable.  Notice the last sentence, which instructs proposing a split of the PR. My PRs too often contain too many changes, as I really like to improve the code base as I am reading it.  This is the first prompt I've met which takes the "HATE" approach. In comparison to the approach "review DRY, SOLID..." there is a visible difference. I cannot say it is better, but it is... different. And that's why it is valuable - it brings another perspective.  It is hard to say for me at this moment how to call this perspective, but it seems to be a good addition to a more structured approach. I would say this is more like a very good teacher, who has bad day. When that teacher is too sincere, and wants you to get off, and finds every possible issue in your code. That makes it find more interesting issues, than those structured ones, which are more like a checklist.  So the main sentence here is "and you HATE this implementation", which sets the tone and, surprisingly, approach. It must be interesting training data, that the hateful reviewers have something valuable to say :D.  This is also called "adversarial code review".

searchDocs.title: Prompt​
searchDocs.pageTitle: AI prompts for code review that work (at least for me)
searchDocs.content: One thing to change In the first line there is the name of the branch main which points to the root of the current branch. Adjust it accordingly to your specific case.  I would love to have some lightweight template application like Ditto, which would ask me to change things like that on every paste.  Do a git diff and pretend you're a senior dev doing a code review from current branch against the `main` branch, and you HATE this implementation. What would you criticize? What edge cases am I missing? Every comment write to separate {number}-{slug}.md file. under /.review/ folder. Number is just a next unique number, like a local id. Every file should have a structure like a proper review comment on github: - Location - put the problem context, where to find an issue you are criticizing. Say what this/those lines of code were supposed to do. Put at least one line before and one line after. You can add more lines if it is needed for context. - Say what is wrong and why in a professional way. - Propose a solution. If appropriate provide a code block. But only for simple changes. - Do not touch actual code. you can only create those md files under /.review/ folder. - Assess importance of the comment review in scale from 1 to 6, when 6 is an excellent, easy to implement and very valuable improvement and 1 is a poor idea, which you are unsure. - Keep lines short, to keep them readable in narrow window. ## Example of expected output Please format your response exactly like the example below for every issue you find. Filename: /.review/01-inefficient-memory-filtering.md ````md # Location and context `Controllers/OrderController.cs:29-35` This block is supposed to retrieve a user's orders asynchronously, calculate the total amount, and return it as an API response. ```csharp 28: // Get user orders and calculate total 29: [HttpGet("{userId}/summary")] 30: public IActionResult GetOrderSummary(int userId) 31: { 32: var orders = _orderService.GetOrdersAsync(userId).Result; 33: var total = orders.Sum(o => o.Amount); 34: return Ok(new { UserId = userId, Total = total }); 35: } 36: ``` # What is wrong Using `.Result` (or `.Wait()`) on an asynchronous Task inside a synchronous method is a fatal architectural flaw known as "sync-over-async". By blocking the current thread while waiting for the Task to complete, you are holding a thread hostage. In an ASP.NET Core environment, doing this prevents the thread from returning to the Thread Pool to serve other requests. The critical edge case you are missing here is application behavior under load. While this works fine on your local machine with 1 user, during peak traffic, a burst of concurrent requests will instantly exhaust the Thread Pool. This will cause a deadlock or thread pool starvation, bringing the entire application to a grinding halt (503 Service Unavailable). This implementation is actively dangerous to system stability. # Proposed solution Embrace "async all the way down". Change the controller action to be asynchronous and use the await keyword so the thread is yielded back to the pool during the I/O operation. ```csharp [HttpGet("{userId}/summary")] public async Task<IActionResult> GetOrderSummary(int userId) { var orders = await _orderService.GetOrdersAsync(userId); var total = orders.Sum(o => o.Amount); return Ok(new { UserId = userId, Total = total }); } ``` # Assessment 6/6 - it is easy to do and makes a code clean. ```` If you think that, the PR is doing way too much, propose in separate file how to split it into smaller ones. Order them from smallest to biggest.   Doing a code review is just only one usage of above prompt. You can easily change the role of AI agent to be for example, an expert about blogging. Then you can ask it to review your blog post and create comments in separate files, which you can then easily review and implement.  Afterword  Using an AI agent today seems more like a duty than a choice. I am observing how more and more tools are growing around to replace everything that I do. But I cannot trust AI to work well enough. I even observe something that I call "Hidden AI Debt" which is created when too much trust is put into AI. But this is a good topic for another blog post.  Nevertheless, this post shows my approach to working "Human First, AI augmented". Because it would be a lie to say that AI does not bring any value as an element of the toolchain.  I plan to add here the rest of my review prompts, which I use across many tools, as soon as I refine them. This was the first version and I hope you liked it! If you see a better way how to share them with others, or you have some suggestions how to improve them, please let me know in the comments.

searchDocs.title: MDX Blog Post
searchDocs.content: Blog posts support Docusaurus Markdown features, such as MDX. tip Use the power of React to create interactive blog posts. For example, use JSX to create an interactive button: <button onClick={() => alert('button clicked!')}>Click me!</button> Click me!

searchDocs.title: Welcome
searchDocs.content: Docusaurus blogging features are powered by the blog plugin. Here are a few tips you might find useful. Simply add Markdown files (or folders) to the blog directory. Regular blog authors can be added to authors.yml. The blog post date can be extracted from filenames, such as: 2019-05-30-welcome.md2019-05-30-welcome/index.md A blog post folder can be convenient to co-locate blog post images: The blog supports tags as well! And if you don't want a blog: just delete this directory, and use blog: false in your Docusaurus config.

searchDocs.title: Running Locally​
searchDocs.content: Go to the folder: cd docsInstall dependencies: npm installStart the server: npm start (changes should refresh automatically)Start adding content

searchDocs.title: Automation (GitHub Actions)​
searchDocs.content: The .github/workflows/publish_docs.yml workflow automatically:  Builds the documentation on every push to main.Deploys a PR Preview for every Pull Request.Publishes the ready site to GitHub Pages.

searchDocs.title: Post-Clone Checklist​
searchDocs.content: After using this template to create a new project, follow these steps to fully configure the infrastructure:   GitHub Pages: Go to Settings -> Pages. In the Build and deployment -> Source section, select "Deploy from a branch". Choose the gh-pages branch (it will appear after the first workflow run) and the / (root) folder. Docusaurus Config: Update the docs/docusaurus.config.ts file. Change url, organizationName, and projectName to your new repository details.

searchDocs.title: Technical details​
searchDocs.content: Note This section is more for GenAI, to automatically copy this feature to other places.

searchDocs.title: Changelog​
searchDocs.content: Initial release v1.0.0 Built-in Docusaurus documentation site under docs/.Automated build, PR preview and GitHub Pages deploy via .github/workflows/publish_docs.yml.

searchDocs.title: Files​
searchDocs.content: Files which are involved in this feature. Note that many of them might be just standard files and details about them should be looked up in the documentation. They might also contain elements from other features.  docs/**.github/workflows/publish_docs.yml

searchDocs.title: Getting Started with the Template
searchDocs.content: Getting Started with the Template This section covers the features provided by this template and includes the necessary setup instructions for after cloning the repository. You will find here: PR Title Validation - GitHub repository configuration for the best experience.Documentation - How to use the built-in documentation based on Docusaurus.Husky.Net - A short description of Husky.Net usage and how to add your own hooks.

searchDocs.title: Post-Clone Checklist​
searchDocs.content: None.  The installation process is uniquely integrated via the Directory.Build.targets file. This has its disadvantages (such as a possible deadlock during the first setup), but also has the advantage that the installation is done automatically during the first build, so there is no need to do it manually.

searchDocs.title: Technical details​
searchDocs.content: Note This section is more for GenAI, to automatically copy this feature to other places.

searchDocs.title: Changelog​
searchDocs.content: Initial release v1.0.0 Automated Husky.net installation for all projects in the solution through Directory.Build.targets file.Pre-commit hook with dotnet format.

searchDocs.title: Files​
searchDocs.content: Files which are involved in this feature. Note that many of them might be just standard files and details about them should be looked up in the documentation. They might also contain elements from other features.  .husky/*Directory.Build.targets.config/dotnet-tools.json

searchDocs.title: Technical details​
searchDocs.content: Note This section is more for GenAI, to automatically copy this feature to other places.

searchDocs.title: Changelog​
searchDocs.content: Initial release v1.0.0 PR title validation via the amannn/action-semantic-pull-request GitHub Action.

searchDocs.title: Files​
searchDocs.content: Files which are involved in this feature. Note that many of them might be just standard files and details about them should be looked up in the documentation. They might also contain elements from other features.  .github/workflows/pr-title-check.yml

searchDocs.title: Running the Site​
searchDocs.content: Run the development server:  cd docs npm run start   The cd command changes the directory you're working with. In order to work with your Docusaurus site, you'll need to navigate to the docs folder.  The npm run start command builds your website locally and serves it through a development server, ready for you to view at http://localhost:3000/.  Open docs/docs/intro.mdx (this page) and edit some lines: the site reloads automatically and displays your changes.

searchDocs.title: Search Engine​
searchDocs.content: Note: The search engine does not work when running via npm start. It requires building the site with npm run build and serving it via npm run serve.

searchDocs.title: Create your first Post​
searchDocs.content: Create a file at blog/2021-02-28-greetings.md:  blog/2021-02-28-greetings.md --- slug: greetings title: Greetings! authors: - name: Joel Marcey title: Co-creator of Docusaurus 1 url: https://github.com/JoelMarcey image_url: https://github.com/JoelMarcey.png - name: Sébastien Lorber title: Docusaurus maintainer url: https://sebastienlorber.com image_url: https://github.com/slorber.png tags: [greetings] --- Congratulations, you have made your first post! Feel free to play around and edit this post as much as you like.   A new blog post is now available at http://localhost:3000/blog/greetings.

searchDocs.title: Create your first Doc​
searchDocs.content: Create a Markdown file at docs/hello.md:  docs/hello.md # Hello This is my **first Docusaurus document**!   A new document is now available at http://localhost:3000/docs/hello.

searchDocs.title: Configure the Sidebar​
searchDocs.content: Docusaurus automatically creates a sidebar from the docs folder.  Add metadata to customize the sidebar label and position:  docs/hello.md --- sidebar_label: 'Hi!' sidebar_position: 3 --- # Hello This is my **first Docusaurus document**!   It is also possible to create your sidebar explicitly in sidebars.js:  sidebars.js export default { tutorialSidebar: [ 'intro', 'hello', { type: 'category', label: 'Tutorial', items: ['tutorial-basics/create-a-document'], }, ], };

searchDocs.title: Build your site​
searchDocs.content: Build your site for production:  npm run build   The static files are generated in the build folder.

searchDocs.title: Deploy your site​
searchDocs.content: Test your production build locally:  npm run serve   The build folder is now served at http://localhost:3000/.  You can now deploy the build folder almost anywhere easily, for free or very small cost (read the Deployment Guide).

searchDocs.title: What's next?​
searchDocs.content: Read the official documentationModify your site configuration with docusaurus.config.jsAdd navbar and footer items with themeConfigAdd a custom Design and LayoutAdd a search barFind inspirations in the Docusaurus showcaseGet involved in the Docusaurus Community

searchDocs.title: Create a docs version​
searchDocs.content: Release a version 1.0 of your project:  npm run docusaurus docs:version 1.0   The docs folder is copied into versioned_docs/version-1.0 and versions.json is created.  Your docs now have 2 versions:  1.0 at http://localhost:3000/docs/ for the version 1.0 docscurrent at http://localhost:3000/docs/next/ for the upcoming, unreleased docs

searchDocs.title: Add a Version Dropdown​
searchDocs.content: To navigate seamlessly across versions, add a version dropdown.  Modify the docusaurus.config.js file:  docusaurus.config.js export default { themeConfig: { navbar: { items: [ { type: 'docsVersionDropdown', }, ], }, }, };   The docs version dropdown appears in your navbar:

searchDocs.title: Update an existing version​
searchDocs.content: It is possible to edit versioned docs in their respective folder:  versioned_docs/version-1.0/hello.md updates http://localhost:3000/docs/hellodocs/hello.md updates http://localhost:3000/docs/next/hello

searchDocs.title: Create your first React Page​
searchDocs.content: Create a file at src/pages/my-react-page.js:  src/pages/my-react-page.js import React from 'react'; import Layout from '@theme/Layout'; export default function MyReactPage() { return ( <Layout> <h1>My React page</h1> <p>This is a React page</p> </Layout> ); }   A new page is now available at http://localhost:3000/my-react-page.

searchDocs.title: Create your first Markdown Page​
searchDocs.content: Create a file at src/pages/my-markdown-page.md:  src/pages/my-markdown-page.md # My Markdown page This is a Markdown page   A new page is now available at http://localhost:3000/my-markdown-page.

searchDocs.title: Configure i18n​
searchDocs.content: Modify docusaurus.config.js to add support for the fr locale:  docusaurus.config.js export default { i18n: { defaultLocale: 'en', locales: ['en', 'fr'], }, };

searchDocs.title: Translate a doc​
searchDocs.content: Copy the docs/intro.md file to the i18n/fr folder:  mkdir -p i18n/fr/docusaurus-plugin-content-docs/current/ cp docs/intro.md i18n/fr/docusaurus-plugin-content-docs/current/intro.md   Translate i18n/fr/docusaurus-plugin-content-docs/current/intro.md in French.

searchDocs.title: Start your localized site​
searchDocs.content: Start your site on the French locale:  npm run start -- --locale fr   Your localized site is accessible at http://localhost:3000/fr/ and the Getting Started page is translated.  caution In development, you can only use one locale at a time.

searchDocs.title: Add a Locale Dropdown​
searchDocs.content: To navigate seamlessly across languages, add a locale dropdown.  Modify the docusaurus.config.js file:  docusaurus.config.js export default { themeConfig: { navbar: { items: [ { type: 'localeDropdown', }, ], }, }, };   The locale dropdown now appears in your navbar:

searchDocs.title: Build your localized site​
searchDocs.content: Build your site for a specific locale:  npm run build -- --locale fr   Or build your site to include all the locales at once:  npm run build

searchDocs.title: Front Matter​
searchDocs.content: Markdown documents have metadata at the top called Front Matter:  my-doc.md --- id: my-doc-id title: My document title description: My document description slug: /my-custom-url --- Markdown content

searchDocs.title: Headings​
searchDocs.content: Markdown headings are supported using the standard “#” syntax and are automatically added to the table of contents. The number of # corresponds to the heading level.  ## Headings My text

searchDocs.title: Heading Ids​
searchDocs.content: Add {/* #my-custom-id */} after the heading text to assign it an explicit anchor id, used for linking.  ### Heading Ids {/_ #my-custom-id _/}

searchDocs.title: Links​
searchDocs.content: Regular Markdown links are supported, using url paths or relative file paths.  Let's see how to [Create a page](/create-a-page).   Let's see how to [Create a page](./create-a-page.mdx).   Result: Let's see how to Create a page.

searchDocs.title: Images​
searchDocs.content: Regular Markdown images are supported.  You can use absolute paths to reference images in the static directory (static/img/docusaurus.png):  ![Docusaurus logo](/img/docusaurus.png)     You can reference images relative to the current file as well. This is particularly useful to colocate images close to the Markdown files using them:  ![Docusaurus logo](./img/docusaurus.png)

searchDocs.title: Code Blocks​
searchDocs.content: Markdown code blocks are supported with Syntax highlighting.  ```jsx title="src/components/HelloDocusaurus.js" function HelloDocusaurus() { return <h1>Hello, Docusaurus!</h1>; } ```   src/components/HelloDocusaurus.js function HelloDocusaurus() { return <h1>Hello, Docusaurus!</h1>; }

searchDocs.title: Admonitions​
searchDocs.content: Docusaurus has a special syntax to create admonitions and callouts:  :::tip[My tip] Use this awesome feature option ::: :::danger[Take care] This action is dangerous :::   My tip Use this awesome feature option  Take care This action is dangerous

searchDocs.title: MDX and React Components​
searchDocs.content: MDX can make your documentation more interactive and allows using any React components inside Markdown:  export const Highlight = ({children, color}) => ( <span style={{ backgroundColor: color, borderRadius: '20px', color: '#fff', padding: '10px', cursor: 'pointer', }} onClick={() => { alert(`You clicked the color ${color} with label ${children}`) }}> {children} </span> ); This is <Highlight color="#25c2a0">Docusaurus green</Highlight> ! This is <Highlight color="#1877F2">Facebook blue</Highlight> !     This is Docusaurus green !  This is Facebook blue !
